does: This paper introduces UniGuard, a multimodal safety guardrail designed to defend MLLMs against jailbreak attacks that exploit both visual and textual inputs. The method trains a guardrail component that jointly analyzes unimodal signals (isolated text or image features) and cross-modal interactions to identify and prevent harmful generations. The guardrail operates as a lightweight inference-time filter that can be applied to any MLLM without requiring model retraining, achieving this through optimization on a toxic corpus to minimize the probability of producing inappropriate responses. Experiments across multiple state-of-the-art MLLMs (LLaVA, Gemini Pro, GPT-4o, MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP) show strong generalization across different attack strategies and modalities while preserving normal vision-language understanding performance.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing safety vulnerabilities in large language models, particularly the emerging threat of multimodal jailbreak attacks that leverage the combination of visual and textual adversarial inputs. It extends prior work on unimodal safety mechanisms for text-only LLMs and vision-only systems by recognizing that cross-modal interactions create new attack surfaces not fully covered by independent modality checks. The paper responds to demonstrations that standard MLLM safety training is insufficient against coordinated multimodal attacks, building on foundations in adversarial robustness and safety guardrail design that have proven effective in simpler settings. It leverages insights from multimodal representation learning about how vision and language information integrates in neural systems.
enables: UniGuard opens pathways for more robust deployment of MLLMs in real-world applications where safety is critical, establishing that universal guardrails can protect multiple model architectures without proprietary access. The approach enables future work on understanding the mechanisms by which cross-modal attacks succeed and potentially designing MLLMs that are inherently more resistant to such attacks. It provides a foundation for developing threat models that account for multimodal attack composition, potentially informing how future models should be trained or evaluated. The generalizability across models suggests that community-wide safety standards for multimodal systems might be achievable, and the lightweight inference-time application could facilitate adoption of safety mechanisms in production systems, laying groundwork for industry-standard safety requirements in vision-language AI deployment.
